comment.body:
Only thing I disagree with you on is a boogeyman is a false threat. China is a real “threat”, and I don’t know where this tit for tat will end? If we continue on this trajectory, it seems very probable that a large majority of the population ends up in shoe box sized apartments eating cricket powder delivered by debt financed DoorDash (at 10% apy) while binge watching Netflix to escape. Especially if the population somehow continues to grow.

At the exact time we need to slow down and figure this shit out is the exact time everything starts to exponentiate. I guess we figured out the nukes, maybe we will here… but at what cost along the way? Feels like we are sleep walking into a Blackmirror episode.

Was thinking about this all last night. Probably a more proper convo to have after hours. So will shut it. But definitely would like to understand how this is going to affect equity prices, government spending, market breadth, etc. As this is a phenomenon that will be unwinding for the next decade. Doesn’t seem like it will be a smooth ride to say the least.

comment.body:
Hi Alma, something for Q&A. I’ve been trying to wrap my head around the Mean Reversion models and improve my own setups. In your Intraday Post from February 28, if I understood correctly, you referred to a short-term Mean Reversion model (based on roughly 1-month data) with an adjusted polynomial trendline. Your comment back then was: "But as long as I don’t see another new white dot (inflection point), I’m not convinced about the strength of the move more than a bounce."
We also discussed, in that post and earlier ones, a longer-term Mean Reversion model (based on about 1 year of data) with an adjusted linear trendline. I think you mentioned in a chat a few days ago that we currently can’t rely on the longer-term signals because, over the bigger picture, we’re not in a Mean Reversion regime.
So, I’ve tried reconstructing these models myself (using SPY closing prices): For my longer-term model, I was getting “BUY signals” between February 25 and March 11, calculated based on extreme deviations from the adjusted trendline or a computed mean (e.g., 20-day). However, based on your comments and my own HURST calculations, I mostly disregarded those signals. From February 28 to March 13, my 3-month Hurst Exponent showed an extremely steep slope, pointing to a Trending Market regime - consistent with your remarks in different Intraday Posts.
Right now, my 3-month Hurst Exponent still shows no real signs of exiting the Trending Market regime (although the 10-day slope is now slightly negative). The polynomial trendline in my short-term model has now hit an inflection point (i.e., after a brief sideways phase, it’s starting to trend slightly upward).
Your current bias is clear and makes sense to me. But at what point would we consider the momentum in the short-term model as “strength” rather than just a “bounce” (liquidity reinjection)? Given the Trending Market regime (e.g. based on the 3-month Hurst), should we still classify any short-term positive momentum as merely a “bounce”?
Unfortunately, I can only run my models on daily data due to limited resources. At what data granularity do ADF tests or the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck equation start making sense? Could daily data over three months still provide meaningful insights into short-term “trend reversals”?
